speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: ’Tis strange, my Theseus, that these lovers speak of.

speaker: THESEUS
text: More strange than true. I never may believe

speaker: THESEUS
text: These antique fables nor these fairy toys.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Lovers and madmen have such seething brains,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Such shaping fantasies, that apprehend

speaker: THESEUS
text: More than cool reason ever comprehends.

speaker: THESEUS
text: The lunatic, the lover, and the poet

speaker: THESEUS
text: Are of imagination all compact.

speaker: THESEUS
text: One sees more devils than vast hell can hold:

speaker: THESEUS
text: That is the madman. The lover, all as frantic,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Sees Helen’s beauty in a brow of Egypt.

speaker: THESEUS
text: The poet’s eye, in a fine frenzy rolling,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Doth glance from heaven to Earth, from Earth to heaven,

speaker: THESEUS
text: And as imagination bodies forth

speaker: THESEUS
text: The forms of things unknown, the poet’s pen

speaker: THESEUS
text: Turns them to shapes and gives to airy nothing

speaker: THESEUS
text: A local habitation and a name.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Such tricks hath strong imagination

speaker: THESEUS
text: That, if it would but apprehend some joy,

speaker: THESEUS
text: It comprehends some bringer of that joy.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Or in the night, imagining some fear,

speaker: THESEUS
text: How easy is a bush supposed a bear!

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: But all the story of the night told over,

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: And all their minds transfigured so together,

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: More witnesseth than fancy’s images

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: And grows to something of great constancy,

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: But, howsoever, strange and admirable.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Here come the lovers full of joy and mirth.—

speaker: THESEUS
text: Joy, gentle friends! Joy and fresh days of love

speaker: THESEUS
text: Accompany your hearts!

speaker: LYSANDER
text: More than to us

speaker: LYSANDER
text: Wait in your royal walks, your board, your bed!

speaker: THESEUS
text: Come now, what masques, what dances shall we have

speaker: THESEUS
text: To wear away this long age of three hours

speaker: THESEUS
text: Between our after-supper and bedtime?

speaker: THESEUS
text: Where is our usual manager of mirth?

speaker: THESEUS
text: What revels are in hand? Is there no play

speaker: THESEUS
text: To ease the anguish of a torturing hour?

speaker: THESEUS
text: Call Philostrate.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Here, mighty Theseus.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Say what abridgment have you for this evening,

speaker: THESEUS
text: What masque, what music? How shall we beguile

speaker: THESEUS
text: The lazy time if not with some delight?

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: There is a brief how many sports are ripe.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Make choice of which your Highness will see first.

speaker: THESEUS
text: “The battle with the Centaurs, to be sung

speaker: THESEUS
text: By an Athenian eunuch to the harp.”

speaker: THESEUS
text: We’ll none of that. That have I told my love

speaker: THESEUS
text: In glory of my kinsman Hercules.

speaker: THESEUS
text: “The riot of the tipsy Bacchanals,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Tearing the Thracian singer in their rage.”

speaker: THESEUS
text: That is an old device, and it was played

speaker: THESEUS
text: When I from Thebes came last a conqueror.

speaker: THESEUS
text: “The thrice-three Muses mourning for the death

speaker: THESEUS
text: Of learning, late deceased in beggary.”

speaker: THESEUS
text: That is some satire, keen and critical,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Not sorting with a nuptial ceremony.

speaker: THESEUS
text: “A tedious brief scene of young Pyramus

speaker: THESEUS
text: And his love Thisbe, very tragical mirth.”

speaker: THESEUS
text: “Merry” and “tragical”? “Tedious” and “brief”?

speaker: THESEUS
text: That is hot ice and wondrous strange snow!

speaker: THESEUS
text: How shall we find the concord of this discord?

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: A play there is, my lord, some ten words long

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: (Which is as brief as I have known a play),

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: But by ten words, my lord, it is too long,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Which makes it tedious; for in all the play,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: There is not one word apt, one player fitted.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: And tragical, my noble lord, it is.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: For Pyramus therein doth kill himself,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Which, when I saw rehearsed, I must confess,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Made mine eyes water; but more merry tears

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: The passion of loud laughter never shed.

speaker: THESEUS
text: What are they that do play it?

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Hard-handed men that work in Athens here,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Which never labored in their minds till now,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: And now have toiled their unbreathed memories

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: With this same play, against your nuptial.

speaker: THESEUS
text: And we will hear it.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: No, my noble lord,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: It is not for you. I have heard it over,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: And it is nothing, nothing in the world,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Unless you can find sport in their intents,

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: Extremely stretched and conned with cruel pain

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: To do you service.

speaker: THESEUS
text: I will hear that play,

speaker: THESEUS
text: For never anything can be amiss

speaker: THESEUS
text: When simpleness and duty tender it.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Go, bring them in—and take your places, ladies.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: I love not to see wretchedness o’ercharged,

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: And duty in his service perishing.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Why, gentle sweet, you shall see no such thing.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: He says they can do nothing in this kind.

speaker: THESEUS
text: The kinder we, to give them thanks for nothing.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Our sport shall be to take what they mistake;

speaker: THESEUS
text: And what poor duty cannot do, noble respect

speaker: THESEUS
text: Takes it in might, not merit.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Where I have come, great clerks have purposèd

speaker: THESEUS
text: To greet me with premeditated welcomes,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Where I have seen them shiver and look pale,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Make periods in the midst of sentences,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Throttle their practiced accent in their fears,

speaker: THESEUS
text: And in conclusion dumbly have broke off,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Not paying me a welcome. Trust me, sweet,

speaker: THESEUS
text: Out of this silence yet I picked a welcome,

speaker: THESEUS
text: And in the modesty of fearful duty,

speaker: THESEUS
text: I read as much as from the rattling tongue

speaker: THESEUS
text: Of saucy and audacious eloquence.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Love, therefore, and tongue-tied simplicity

speaker: THESEUS
text: In least speak most, to my capacity.

speaker: PHILOSTRATE
text: So please your Grace, the Prologue is addressed.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Let him approach.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: If we offend, it is with our goodwill.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: That you should think we come not to offend,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: But with goodwill. To show our simple skill,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: That is the true beginning of our end.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Consider, then, we come but in despite.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: We do not come, as minding to content you,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Our true intent is. All for your delight

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: We are not here. That you should here repent you,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: The actors are at hand, and, by their show,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: You shall know all that you are like to know.

speaker: THESEUS
text: This fellow doth not stand upon points.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: He hath rid his prologue like a rough colt;

speaker: LYSANDER
text: he knows not the stop. A good moral, my lord: it is

speaker: LYSANDER
text: not enough to speak, but to speak true.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: Indeed he hath played on this prologue like

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: a child on a recorder—a sound, but not in

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: government.

speaker: THESEUS
text: His speech was like a tangled chain—nothing

speaker: THESEUS
text: impaired, but all disordered. Who is next?

speaker: QUINCE
text: Gentles, perchance you wonder at this show.

speaker: QUINCE
text: But wonder on, till truth make all things plain.

speaker: QUINCE
text: This man is Pyramus, if you would know.

speaker: QUINCE
text: This beauteous lady Thisbe is certain.

speaker: QUINCE
text: This man with lime and roughcast doth present

speaker: QUINCE
text: “Wall,” that vile wall which did these lovers sunder;

speaker: QUINCE
text: And through Wall’s chink, poor souls, they are content

speaker: QUINCE
text: To whisper, at the which let no man wonder.

speaker: QUINCE
text: This man, with lantern, dog, and bush of thorn,

speaker: QUINCE
text: Presenteth “Moonshine,” for, if you will know,

speaker: QUINCE
text: By moonshine did these lovers think no scorn

speaker: QUINCE
text: To meet at Ninus’ tomb, there, there to woo.

speaker: QUINCE
text: This grisly beast (which “Lion” hight by name)

speaker: QUINCE
text: The trusty Thisbe coming first by night

speaker: QUINCE
text: Did scare away or rather did affright;

speaker: QUINCE
text: And, as she fled, her mantle she did fall,

speaker: QUINCE
text: Which Lion vile with bloody mouth did stain.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Anon comes Pyramus, sweet youth and tall,

speaker: QUINCE
text: And finds his trusty Thisbe’s mantle slain.

speaker: QUINCE
text: Whereat, with blade, with bloody blameful blade,

speaker: QUINCE
text: He bravely broached his boiling bloody breast.

speaker: QUINCE
text: And Thisbe, tarrying in mulberry shade,

speaker: QUINCE
text: His dagger drew, and died. For all the rest,

speaker: QUINCE
text: Let Lion, Moonshine, Wall, and lovers twain

speaker: QUINCE
text: At large discourse, while here they do remain.

speaker: THESEUS
text: I wonder if the lion be to speak.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: No wonder, my lord. One lion may when

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: many asses do.

speaker: SNOUT
text: In this same interlude it doth befall

speaker: SNOUT
text: That I, one Snout by name, present a wall;

speaker: SNOUT
text: And such a wall as I would have you think

speaker: SNOUT
text: That had in it a crannied hole or chink,

speaker: SNOUT
text: Through which the lovers, Pyramus and Thisbe,

speaker: SNOUT
text: Did whisper often, very secretly.

speaker: SNOUT
text: This loam, this roughcast, and this stone doth show

speaker: SNOUT
text: That I am that same wall. The truth is so.

speaker: SNOUT
text: And this the cranny is, right and sinister,

speaker: SNOUT
text: Through which the fearful lovers are to whisper.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Would you desire lime and hair to speak

speaker: THESEUS
text: better?

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: It is the wittiest partition that ever I heard

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: discourse, my lord.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Pyramus draws near the wall. Silence.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O grim-looked night! O night with hue so black!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O night, which ever art when day is not!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O night! O night! Alack, alack, alack!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I fear my Thisbe’s promise is forgot.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: And thou, O wall, O sweet, O lovely wall,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: That stand’st between her father’s ground and mine,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thou wall, O wall, O sweet and lovely wall,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Show me thy chink to blink through with mine eyne.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thanks, courteous wall. Jove shield thee well for this.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: But what see I? No Thisbe do I see.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O wicked wall, through whom I see no bliss,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Cursed be thy stones for thus deceiving me!

speaker: THESEUS
text: The wall, methinks, being sensible, should

speaker: THESEUS
text: curse again.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: No, in truth, sir, he should not. “Deceiving

speaker: BOTTOM
text: me” is Thisbe’s cue. She is to enter now, and I am

speaker: BOTTOM
text: to spy her through the wall. You shall see it will fall

speaker: BOTTOM
text: pat as I told you. Yonder she comes.

speaker: FLUTE
text: O wall, full often hast thou heard my moans

speaker: FLUTE
text: For parting my fair Pyramus and me.

speaker: FLUTE
text: My cherry lips have often kissed thy stones,

speaker: FLUTE
text: Thy stones with lime and hair knit up in thee.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I see a voice! Now will I to the chink

speaker: BOTTOM
text: To spy an I can hear my Thisbe’s face.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thisbe?

speaker: FLUTE
text: My love! Thou art my love, I think.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Think what thou wilt, I am thy lover’s grace,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: And, like Limander, am I trusty still.

speaker: FLUTE
text: And I like Helen, till the Fates me kill.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Not Shafalus to Procrus was so true.

speaker: FLUTE
text: As Shafalus to Procrus, I to you.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O kiss me through the hole of this vile wall.

speaker: FLUTE
text: I kiss the wall’s hole, not your lips at all.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Wilt thou at Ninny’s tomb meet me straightway?

speaker: FLUTE
text: ’Tide life, ’tide death, I come without delay.

speaker: SNOUT
text: Thus have I, Wall, my part dischargèd so,

speaker: SNOUT
text: And, being done, thus Wall away doth go.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Now is the wall down between the two

speaker: THESEUS
text: neighbors.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: No remedy, my lord, when walls are so

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: willful to hear without warning.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: This is the silliest stuff that ever I heard.

speaker: THESEUS
text: The best in this kind are but shadows, and

speaker: THESEUS
text: the worst are no worse, if imagination amend

speaker: THESEUS
text: them.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: It must be your imagination, then, and not

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: theirs.

speaker: THESEUS
text: If we imagine no worse of them than they of

speaker: THESEUS
text: themselves, they may pass for excellent men. Here

speaker: THESEUS
text: come two noble beasts in, a man and a lion.

speaker: SNUG
text: You ladies, you whose gentle hearts do fear

speaker: SNUG
text: The smallest monstrous mouse that creeps on floor,

speaker: SNUG
text: May now perchance both quake and tremble here,

speaker: SNUG
text: When lion rough in wildest rage doth roar.

speaker: SNUG
text: Then know that I, as Snug the joiner, am

speaker: SNUG
text: A lion fell, nor else no lion’s dam;

speaker: SNUG
text: For if I should as lion come in strife

speaker: SNUG
text: Into this place, ’twere pity on my life.

speaker: THESEUS
text: A very gentle beast, and of a good

speaker: THESEUS
text: conscience.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: The very best at a beast, my lord, that e’er I

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: saw.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: This lion is a very fox for his valor.

speaker: THESEUS
text: True, and a goose for his discretion.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: Not so, my lord, for his valor cannot carry

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: his discretion, and the fox carries the goose.

speaker: THESEUS
text: His discretion, I am sure, cannot carry his

speaker: THESEUS
text: valor, for the goose carries not the fox. It is well.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Leave it to his discretion, and let us listen to the

speaker: THESEUS
text: Moon.

speaker: STARVELING
text: This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: He should have worn the horns on his

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: head.

speaker: THESEUS
text: He is no crescent, and his horns are invisible

speaker: THESEUS
text: within the circumference.

speaker: STARVELING
text: This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present.

speaker: STARVELING
text: Myself the man i’ th’ moon do seem to be.

speaker: THESEUS
text: This is the greatest error of all the rest; the

speaker: THESEUS
text: man should be put into the lanthorn. How is it else

speaker: THESEUS
text: “the man i’ th’ moon”?

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: He dares not come there for the candle,

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: for you see, it is already in snuff.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: I am aweary of this moon. Would he would

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: change.

speaker: THESEUS
text: It appears by his small light of discretion that

speaker: THESEUS
text: he is in the wane; but yet, in courtesy, in all reason,

speaker: THESEUS
text: we must stay the time.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: Proceed, Moon.

speaker: STARVELING
text: All that I have to say is to tell

speaker: STARVELING
text: you that the lanthorn is the moon, I the man i’ th’

speaker: STARVELING
text: moon, this thornbush my thornbush, and this dog

speaker: STARVELING
text: my dog.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: Why, all these should be in the lanthorn,

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: for all these are in the moon. But silence. Here

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: comes Thisbe.

speaker: FLUTE
text: This is old Ninny’s tomb. Where is my love?

speaker: SNUG
text: O!

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: Well roared, Lion.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Well run, Thisbe.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: Well shone, Moon. Truly, the Moon shines

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: with a good grace.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Well moused, Lion.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: And then came Pyramus.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: And so the lion vanished.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Sweet Moon, I thank thee for thy sunny beams.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I thank thee, Moon, for shining now so bright,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: For by thy gracious, golden, glittering gleams,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: I trust to take of truest Thisbe sight.—

speaker: BOTTOM
text: But stay! O spite!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: But mark, poor knight,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: What dreadful dole is here!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Eyes, do you see!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: How can it be!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O dainty duck! O dear!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thy mantle good—

speaker: BOTTOM
text: What, stained with blood?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Approach, ye Furies fell!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O Fates, come, come,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Cut thread and thrum,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Quail, crush, conclude, and quell!

speaker: THESEUS
text: This passion, and the death of a dear friend,

speaker: THESEUS
text: would go near to make a man look sad.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: Beshrew my heart but I pity the man.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: O, wherefore, Nature, didst thou lions frame,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Since lion vile hath here deflowered my dear,

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Which is—no, no—which was the fairest dame

speaker: BOTTOM
text: That lived, that loved, that liked, that looked with cheer?

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Come, tears, confound!

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Out, sword, and wound

speaker: BOTTOM
text: The pap of Pyramus;

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Ay, that left pap,

speaker: BOTTOM
text:
Where heart doth hop.
                  Pyramus
                   
                  stabs
                   
                  himself
                  .

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Thus die I, thus, thus, thus.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Now am I dead;

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Now am I fled;

speaker: BOTTOM
text: My soul is in the sky.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Tongue, lose thy light!

speaker: BOTTOM
text:
Moon, take thy flight!
                  Moonshine
                   
                  exits
                  .

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Now die, die, die, die, die.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: No die, but an ace for him, for he is but

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: one.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: Less than an ace, man, for he is dead, he is

speaker: LYSANDER
text: nothing.

speaker: THESEUS
text: With the help of a surgeon he might yet

speaker: THESEUS
text: recover and yet prove an ass.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: How chance Moonshine is gone before

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: Thisbe comes back and finds her lover?

speaker: THESEUS
text: She will find him by starlight.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Here she comes, and her passion ends the play.

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: Methinks she should not use a long one for

speaker: HIPPOLYTA
text: such a Pyramus. I hope she will be brief.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: A mote will turn the balance, which Pyramus,

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: which Thisbe, is the better: he for a man, God

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: warrant us; she for a woman, God bless us.

speaker: LYSANDER
text: She hath spied him already with those

speaker: LYSANDER
text: sweet eyes.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: And thus she means, videlicet—

speaker: FLUTE
text: Asleep, my love?

speaker: FLUTE
text: What, dead, my dove?

speaker: FLUTE
text: O Pyramus, arise!

speaker: FLUTE
text: Speak, speak. Quite dumb?

speaker: FLUTE
text: Dead? Dead? A tomb

speaker: FLUTE
text: Must cover thy sweet eyes.

speaker: FLUTE
text: These lily lips,

speaker: FLUTE
text: This cherry nose,

speaker: FLUTE
text: These yellow cowslip cheeks

speaker: FLUTE
text: Are gone, are gone!

speaker: FLUTE
text: Lovers, make moan;

speaker: FLUTE
text: His eyes were green as leeks.

speaker: FLUTE
text: O Sisters Three,

speaker: FLUTE
text: Come, come to me

speaker: FLUTE
text: With hands as pale as milk.

speaker: FLUTE
text: Lay them in gore,

speaker: FLUTE
text: Since you have shore

speaker: FLUTE
text: With shears his thread of silk.

speaker: FLUTE
text: Tongue, not a word!

speaker: FLUTE
text: Come, trusty sword,

speaker: FLUTE
text:
Come, blade, my breast imbrue!
                  Thisbe
                   
                  stabs
                   
                  herself
                  .

speaker: FLUTE
text: And farewell, friends.

speaker: FLUTE
text: Thus Thisbe ends.

speaker: FLUTE
text:
Adieu, adieu, adieu.
                  Thisbe
                   
                  falls
                  .

speaker: THESEUS
text: Moonshine and Lion are left to bury the

speaker: THESEUS
text: dead.

speaker: DEMETRIUS
text: Ay, and Wall too.

speaker: BOTTOM
text: No, I assure you, the wall is down that

speaker: BOTTOM
text: parted their fathers. Will it please you to see the

speaker: BOTTOM
text: Epilogue or to hear a Bergomask dance between

speaker: BOTTOM
text: two of our company?

speaker: THESEUS
text: No epilogue, I pray you. For your play needs

speaker: THESEUS
text: no excuse. Never excuse. For when the players are

speaker: THESEUS
text: all dead, there need none to be blamed. Marry, if

speaker: THESEUS
text: he that writ it had played Pyramus and hanged

speaker: THESEUS
text: himself in Thisbe’s garter, it would have been a fine

speaker: THESEUS
text: tragedy; and so it is, truly, and very notably discharged.

speaker: THESEUS
text: But, come, your Bergomask. Let your

speaker: THESEUS
text: epilogue alone.

speaker: THESEUS
text: The iron tongue of midnight hath told twelve.

speaker: THESEUS
text: Lovers, to bed! ’Tis almost fairy time.

speaker: THESEUS
text: I fear we shall outsleep the coming morn

speaker: THESEUS
text: As much as we this night have overwatched.

speaker: THESEUS
text: This palpable-gross play hath well beguiled

speaker: THESEUS
text: The heavy gait of night. Sweet friends, to bed.

speaker: THESEUS
text: A fortnight hold we this solemnity

speaker: THESEUS
text: In nightly revels and new jollity.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Now the hungry lion roars,

speaker: ROBIN
text: And the wolf behowls the moon,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Whilst the heavy plowman snores,

speaker: ROBIN
text: All with weary task fordone.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Now the wasted brands do glow,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Whilst the screech-owl, screeching loud,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Puts the wretch that lies in woe

speaker: ROBIN
text: In remembrance of a shroud.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Now it is the time of night

speaker: ROBIN
text: That the graves, all gaping wide,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Every one lets forth his sprite

speaker: ROBIN
text: In the church-way paths to glide.

speaker: ROBIN
text: And we fairies, that do run

speaker: ROBIN
text: By the triple Hecate’s team

speaker: ROBIN
text: From the presence of the sun,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Following darkness like a dream,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Now are frolic. Not a mouse

speaker: ROBIN
text: Shall disturb this hallowed house.

speaker: ROBIN
text: I am sent with broom before,

speaker: ROBIN
text: To sweep the dust behind the door.

speaker: OBERON
text: Through the house give glimmering light,

speaker: OBERON
text: By the dead and drowsy fire.

speaker: OBERON
text: Every elf and fairy sprite,

speaker: OBERON
text: Hop as light as bird from brier,

speaker: OBERON
text: And this ditty after me,

speaker: OBERON
text: Sing and dance it trippingly.

speaker: TITANIA
text: First rehearse your song by rote,

speaker: TITANIA
text: To each word a warbling note.

speaker: TITANIA
text: Hand in hand, with fairy grace,

speaker: TITANIA
text: Will we sing and bless this place.

speaker: OBERON
text: Now, until the break of day,

speaker: OBERON
text: Through this house each fairy stray.

speaker: OBERON
text: To the best bride-bed will we,

speaker: OBERON
text: Which by us shall blessèd be,

speaker: OBERON
text: And the issue there create

speaker: OBERON
text: Ever shall be fortunate.

speaker: OBERON
text: So shall all the couples three

speaker: OBERON
text: Ever true in loving be,

speaker: OBERON
text: And the blots of Nature’s hand

speaker: OBERON
text: Shall not in their issue stand.

speaker: OBERON
text: Never mole, harelip, nor scar,

speaker: OBERON
text: Nor mark prodigious, such as are

speaker: OBERON
text: Despisèd in nativity,

speaker: OBERON
text: Shall upon their children be.

speaker: OBERON
text: With this field-dew consecrate

speaker: OBERON
text: Every fairy take his gait,

speaker: OBERON
text: And each several chamber bless,

speaker: OBERON
text: Through this palace, with sweet peace.

speaker: OBERON
text: And the owner of it blest,

speaker: OBERON
text: Ever shall in safety rest.

speaker: OBERON
text: Trip away. Make no stay.

speaker: OBERON
text: Meet me all by break of day.

speaker: ROBIN
text: If we shadows have offended,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Think but this and all is mended:

speaker: ROBIN
text: That you have but slumbered here

speaker: ROBIN
text: While these visions did appear.

speaker: ROBIN
text: And this weak and idle theme,

speaker: ROBIN
text: No more yielding but a dream,

speaker: ROBIN
text: Gentles, do not reprehend.

speaker: ROBIN
text: If you pardon, we will mend.

speaker: ROBIN
text: And, as I am an honest Puck,

speaker: ROBIN
text: If we have unearnèd luck

speaker: ROBIN
text: Now to ’scape the serpent’s tongue,

speaker: ROBIN
text: We will make amends ere long.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Else the Puck a liar call.

speaker: ROBIN
text: So good night unto you all.

speaker: ROBIN
text: Give me your hands, if we be friends,

speaker: ROBIN
text: And Robin shall restore amends.
